intro:
David (Hebrew Dawid, "beloved") was the youngest of Jesse's sons, a shepherd of Bethlehem who became the greatest king of Israel and the man with whom God made an everlasting dynastic covenant. His reign (approximately 1010–970 BC) set the theological template of covenant kingship that shapes all subsequent messianic hope; the very title Messiah ("anointed one") looks back to the anointed son of Jesse, and the New Testament opens by naming Jesus "the son of David" (Matthew 1:1).

David is chosen when Samuel, sent to Jesse, passes over the older sons because "the LORD looks on the heart" (1 Samuel 16:7) and anoints the shepherd boy (1 Samuel 16:13). He rises to fame by killing the Philistine champion Goliath, declaring that "the battle is the LORD's" (1 Samuel 17:47). His success provokes the jealousy of King Saul, and David spends years as a fugitive, twice sparing Saul's life rather than lifting his hand against "the LORD's anointed" (1 Samuel 24:6). After Saul falls on Mount Gilboa, David is made king over Judah at Hebron and, seven years later, over all Israel (2 Samuel 5:3). He captures the Jebusite stronghold of Jerusalem, makes it his capital, and brings the ark there, uniting the nation's worship and throne in one city (2 Samuel 6:12).

The covenant theology of David's reign turns on 2 Samuel 7:12–16: when David proposes to build a house (temple) for God, God promises instead to build David a "house" (dynasty), vowing that his offspring's throne will be established forever. This Davidic covenant becomes the seedbed of messianic prophecy throughout the Psalms and Prophets. Yet David is no idealized figure: his adultery with Bathsheba and the murder of Uriah (2 Samuel 11) bring Nathan's rebuke and lifelong turmoil — the sword never departs from his house, and the rebellion of Absalom nearly costs him the throne. His response to sin, however, is the deep contrition voiced in Psalm 51:1–4, the prayer of a broken and contrite heart.

Scripture remembers David as "a man after God's own heart" (1 Samuel 13:14; Acts 13:22) — not sinless, but wholly devoted to the LORD and quick to repent. He is the singer of Israel, traditionally tied to the Psalter, whose laments and praises gave the church its prayer book. The prophets keep the Davidic hope alive through exile, promising a coming "Branch" and a shepherd-king "my servant David" (Ezekiel 34:23–24). The Gospels present Jesus as the long-awaited son of David, and Revelation names Him "the Root and the Descendant of David" (Revelation 22:16), in whom the everlasting throne promised at 2 Samuel 7:16 is secured.
